rd.title: Protein-Templated Biomineralization at Manufacturing Scale: Bridging the Mechanical Property Gap
rd.problem_statement: Biomineralization processes mediated by proteins produce materials with remarkable hierarchical organization and toughness in nature (nacre: 140 MPa tensile strength, bone: 130 MPa). Laboratory demonstrations have replicated protein-mineral co-assembly at millimeter scales but exhibit mechanical properties with 40-60% variation and mean tensile strengths below 80 MPa. Scaling to centimeter dimensions causes catastrophic mechanical failure, suggesting fundamental physical limitations in protein diffusion kinetics, mineralization front propagation, or defect nucleation that are masked at small scales. The core problem is not replicating the chemistry but maintaining structural coherence across length scales where thermodynamic, kinetic, and mechanical forces operate under different regimes.
known_constraints: Protein diffusion limits (10^-7 cm²/s in mineralization media) create concentration gradients at scales >5mm
Mineral nucleation is stochastic with correlation lengths ~100 μm, causing cumulative defect density
Osmotic pressure differentials (>2 MPa) during mineralization induce internal stress fields
Manufacturing timescales (hours) conflict with protein stability half-lives (4-12 hours at process temperatures)
Capital cost constraints require throughput >10 kg/hour for economic viability
rd.bottleneck: The mineralization front velocity cannot be decoupled from protein template organization: accelerating mineral growth to manufacturing speeds disrupts the protein scaffolding before mechanical integration occurs, while maintaining proper protein assembly extends manufacturing time beyond protein functional lifetime. This is fundamentally a rate-coupling problem where the two critical timescales (protein ordering and mineral deposition) cannot be independently controlled with current approaches.
commitments: Protein templating is necessary (not merely sufficient) for achieving target mechanical properties—purely inorganic hierarchical structures lack the interfacial toughening mechanisms that proteins provide through sacrificial bonding and energy dissipation pathways.

The lab-to-manufacturing failure is primarily a kinetic control problem, not a thermodynamic or materials discovery problem—the right structures are thermodynamically accessible but current processes cannot guide the system along viable kinetic pathways at scale.

Mechanical property variance scales super-linearly with sample dimension due to defect percolation dynamics—a 10x increase in characteristic length produces >30x increase in variance, indicating critical phase transition behavior around centimeter scales.
hidden_variables: Electric field gradients generated by charged protein domains during assembly (unmeasured in all published protocols)
Convective flow patterns in mineralization baths at scales >10mm (assumed negligible, likely significant)
Time-dependent viscoelastic response of the protein phase during mineral infiltration (measured only at equilibrium)
Subsurface pH gradients during CO2/carbonate mineralization (point measurements only)
Mechanical pre-stress accumulation during growth (inferred indirectly, never directly quantified)
Batch-to-batch variation in protein conformational distributions (single-state models dominate literature)
rd.success_criteria: A manufacturing process producing continuous material (>10 cm minimum dimension) with tensile strength >100 MPa, coefficient of variation <20%, at throughput >1 kg/hour, using proteins that constitute <15% mass fraction. The process must be demonstrable with at least two distinct protein-mineral systems (generalizability requirement) and show predictable property scaling relationships with process parameters. Economic threshold: material cost <$50/kg including protein synthesis/purification.
frontier_questions: Can we measure and control the 'mechanical information flow' through a growing mineralized matrix—the rate at which stress fields communicate through partially mineralized protein networks?
Does there exist a characteristic dimensionless number (analogous to Reynolds or Péclet) that predicts the transition from successful to failed biomineralization scaling?
Are there protein topologies that remain functional under oscillating chemical potentials, enabling cyclic manufacturing processes that periodically reset kinetic traps?

What is the actual causal mechanism linking protein secondary structure dynamics to mineral crystal orientation—do proteins template through geometric constraint, electric field alignment, or stereochemical recognition?
Can spatially programmed protein degradation create 'sacrificial zones' that control defect propagation without compromising bulk mechanical properties?
Is the manufacturing scale barrier a fundamental physical limit or an artifact of spatially uniform processing conditions?

dec.governing_tension: Horizontal trust infrastructure promises economies of scale and cross-programme coherence, but requires upfront coordination costs and deferred programme-level wins. Vertical embedding delivers faster programme-specific results and clearer accountability, but risks duplication and fragmented standards. The 12-month pressure window makes horizontal investment politically expensive: benefits accrue beyond the accountability horizon while early costs are visible.
dec.failure_pathway: Most likely failure: Fund horizontal infrastructure with nominal buy-in from programmes, but no single empowered owner. Programmes build parallel vertical solutions when horizontal delivery lags or doesn't meet specialized needs. By month 18, ARIA operates three incompatible trust systems, has spent £2M on horizontal infrastructure used by nobody, and programme directors publicly blame 'central bureaucracy' for delays. The hybrid compromise becomes the worst of both worlds.
dec.state_of_art: Modern research infrastructure reveals a consistent pattern: horizontal platforms succeed only when a powerful customer programme co-funds and co-designs from day one, ensuring the platform solves a real problem rather than an imagined general need. Vertical-first with planned federation (build programme solutions, then extract common components) outperforms grand unified designs in time-to-value and adoption rates. The political economy of shared services in research organizations consistently favors visible programme wins over invisible infrastructure, making sustained horizontal investment fragile under leadership changes or budget pressure.
commitments: Horizontal trust infrastructure will fail without a single empowered owner who can compel programme adoption and has authority to deny programmes the option of building parallel vertical solutions.
The 12-month political pressure window makes pure horizontal investment effectively infeasible: the accountability cycle is shorter than the minimum time to demonstrate cross-programme value.

Vertical-first within Scaling Trust, with contractual commitment to open-source reusable components by month 18, delivers higher expected value than day-one horizontal investment under current governance constraints.

key_sources.title: Horizontal infrastructure adoption dynamics in research organizations
key_sources.finding: Shared platforms without anchor customer programmes experience low adoption and high abandonment rates

key_sources.title: Time-to-value in centralized vs distributed infrastructure models
key_sources.finding: Vertical-first with planned federation typically delivers usable capability 6-9 months faster than horizontal-first designs

key_sources.title: Political economy of research infrastructure funding
key_sources.finding: Infrastructure investment is vulnerable to defunding when programme directors face short-term delivery pressure and can credibly blame infrastructure delays

key_sources.title: Governance requirements for successful shared service platforms
key_sources.finding: Effective horizontal platforms require an empowered owner with veto authority over parallel programme investments
